description.s: Investigate how Claude can make live changes to running Hobson without manual intervention (no import/export, no approval steps).

Currently Claude Code edits exported JSON files in src/items/, then the user manually imports them into Hobson. This works but requires constant context-switching and manual steps. The goal is for the user to describe a change and have Claude execute it directly against the running system.
Key constraint: no extra processes beyond Hobson (or its host browser/Tauri shell) and Claude.
A library item wraps the Claude API (fetch to api.anthropic.com with tool use). Tools map directly to Hobson's API. Conversations are items. Any view or REPL command can invoke Claude.
Zero extra processes. AI is a native capability within the item system.
Claude can see everything Hobson sees — the call originates from inside the item system.
This is a separate Claude instance, not the same Claude Code used for development.
API key stored in the browser.
Option B: Claude Code via MCP

Hobson exposes its API as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. Claude Code connects as a client and can call tools natively. This means the same Claude the user is already talking to can reach directly into the running system.
One Claude for everything — development and live changes.
MCP is a standard protocol. Claude Code already supports it natively.
Browsers cannot expose stdio or listen on ports. Requires either a small bridge process or a Tauri shell.
One Claude (Code) that reaches into Hobson via MCP. The transport problem has two solutions depending on Hobson's runtime:

A small (~50 line) Node script translates between MCP stdio and a WebSocket connected to Hobson in the browser. The bridge is stateless plumbing with no application logic. A Hobson library item (mcp-server-lib) opens the WebSocket listener and handles incoming tool calls by dispatching to Hobson's API.

If Hobson moves to a Tauri desktop shell, the transport layer (stdio or local HTTP/SSE) lives in Tauri's Rust backend (~100 lines, built once, never changes). The MCP tool logic lives in a standard Hobson item that receives messages via Tauri's IPC bridge. Claude Code connects via SSE to the Tauri app — no separate process needed.
The tools are thin wrappers around Hobson's existing API:
is the escape hatch — anything without a dedicated tool can be done via eval.
enables Claude to see rendered output (including code that executes during rendering) by capturing a screenshot and returning it as an image for Claude's vision capabilities.
Items can contain code that executes during rendering — Claude needs to see the rendered result, not just the raw JSON. The render_item tool:
Renders the item into a container using Hobson's existing viewport rendering pipeline.
Waits for render to settle (async code, network loads, etc.).
Captures the container via html2canvas (loaded as a Hobson library item) → base64 PNG.
Returns the image to Claude, who can assess it via vision.
This closes the feedback loop: Claude makes a change, sees the rendered result, and can self-correct — all within a single conversation turn.

The bridge process violates the 'no extra processes' constraint but is trivial. Tauri eliminates it but is a larger commitment. Does Hobson move to Tauri for other reasons (filesystem access, offline, system integration) that make this a natural step?
For Option A (in-browser), the key lives in a Hobson item or config. For Option B (Claude Code), Claude Code manages its own key. If both are eventually needed, key storage should be unified.
Should Claude always execute changes immediately, or show a preview for code changes? This is a policy choice that can be tuned later, but the tool schema should support both modes.

How much of Hobson's state should Claude see in each conversation? All items? Just the relevant ones? The tool-use approach naturally scopes this — Claude fetches what it needs — but system prompt context matters too.
Claude Code builds the MCP integration, but once built, it largely replaces the file-editing workflow. How does Claude Code fix the MCP integration itself if it breaks?
